does: This paper introduces two new behavioral alignment metrics for comparing AI and human decision-making systems without requiring expensive neural data collection. The authors propose misclassification agreement, which quantifies how similarly two systems err on identical instances, and class-level error similarity, which compares the error distributions across categories. Rather than measuring internal representational similarity, these metrics operate purely on observable errors and predictions. The paper argues that error patterns provide meaningful signals about whether AI systems process information in ways aligned with human cognition, offering a practical alternative to representational alignment approaches that demand difficult-to-obtain human brain recordings or detailed introspection data.
connects: This work extends the broader program of measuring alignment between AI and human cognition, building on representational alignment research that compares internal neural representations across systems. The paper directly responds to limitations in RA approaches—their expense and difficulty in acquiring human neural data—by proposing behavioral alternatives. It connects to interpretability and explainability literature seeking to establish trust in opaque AI systems, and draws on comparative cognitive science traditions that infer mental processes from behavioral patterns. The focus on error analysis also relates to work on machine learning calibration, disagreement detection, and the study of systematic biases in both human and artificial decision-makers.
enables: These behavioral metrics open pathways for large-scale empirical assessment of whether AI systems make errors in human-like ways, potentially informing training procedures that encourage alignment. The framework could facilitate auditing and certification of AI systems for deployment in high-stakes domains by quantifying decision-making similarity without prohibitive data collection. Future work might develop these metrics into formal measures of trustworthiness or use error alignment as a training objective. The approach also enables comparative studies across diverse AI architectures and human populations to understand what factors drive convergent or divergent error patterns, potentially revealing which aspects of human reasoning are replicable and which require explicit architectural choices.
